title: There's No Multiply Instruction
lesson:
The PDP-8 can add (TAD) but it cannot multiply. There's no MUL in the hardware. Just like the Baby built addition out of subtraction, you build multiplication out of addition: to work out 6 times 4, you add 6 to a running total four times. A loop with a counter does exactly that.

This is a pattern you'll meet again and again, in this game and in real life. The hardware gives you a small set of basic moves, and software combines them into everything else. A 1965 minicomputer multiplied this way millions of times a second, fast enough to be useful, but also a reminder of why later machines added hardware multipliers, and why we eventually wanted far more powerful processors.
prompt: Work out 6 times 4, which is 24, by adding 6 to a running total four times with an ISZ loop. Leave 24 in the accumulator.
starterCode:
*200
        CLA CLL
        TAD MFOUR   / AC = -4  (loop 4 times)
        DCA CTR
        DCA SUM     / SUM = 0
LOOP,   TAD SUM
        TAD SIX     / SUM = SUM + 6
        DCA SUM
        ISZ CTR     / count this pass, skip the JMP when done
        JMP LOOP
        TAD SUM     / AC = 24
        HLT
SUM,    0
CTR,    0
MFOUR,  -4
SIX,    6
hints: Multiplying 6 by 4 just means adding 6 four times.
Each pass: load SUM, TAD SIX, store it back to SUM. Use a -4 counter with ISZ to run the loop four times.
After the loop, TAD SUM puts the product (24) in AC.
successText: AC must equal 24 (that's 6 times 4), built from repeated addition.
reference:
*200
 CLA CLL
 TAD MFOUR
 DCA CTR
 DCA SUM
LOOP, TAD SUM
 TAD SIX
 DCA SUM
 ISZ CTR
 JMP LOOP
 TAD SUM
 HLT
SUM, 0
CTR, 0
MFOUR, -4
SIX, 6
